attributes.stem:
A 81-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with acute confusion and shortness of breath. She has a medical history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Her regular medications include tiotropium and salbutamol inhalers. The son who lives with her demands that she would want to continue living with whatever medical means are required. However, her daughter presents a legally valid instruction directive completed six months ago when the patient had decision-making capacity. The directive specifically states that she does not want intubation under any circumstances. Her arterial blood gas shows severe type 2 respiratory failure.

On examination, she appears distressed with a pulse rate of 120/min, blood pressure 160/95 mmHg, respiratory rate 32/min, SpO2 85% on 15 L/min oxygen via non-rebreather mask, and temperature 37.8°C. She is unable to complete sentences and is becoming increasingly drowsy.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Proceed with intubation as it is medically indicated
Contact the Public Guardian for direction
attributes.explanation:
This case highlights the ethical and legal principles surrounding instruction directives (also known as advance directives or living wills). The patient has a valid instruction directive that specifically refuses intubation, completed when she had decision-making capacity. However not all family or friends would be acceptive of the advanced care directives as in this case which can make it difficult. Patients should be encouraged to discuss their wishes and values of their advanced care directives with their family and close friends to prevent misunderstandings at later times.

Non-invasive ventilation represents the most appropriate management option as it respects the patient's autonomy while still providing respiratory support. This approach aligns with the directive's specific refusal of intubation while attempting to treat the underlying condition.

Proceeding with intubation would directly contravene the patient's explicit wishes as documented in their valid instruction directive, despite the son's wishes. Healthcare professionals have a legal and ethical obligation to follow valid instruction directives unless circumstances have changed substantially since the directive was made.

Contacting the Public Guardian is not necessary when there is a valid instruction directive in place. The Public Guardian becomes involved when there is no directive and no appointed substitute decision-maker.

Seeking family consent for intubation would be inappropriate as the instruction directive takes precedence over family wishes. The directive represents the patient's own decision made with capacity, and family members cannot override it.

Awaiting medical tribunal decision is not necessary when there is a clear, valid instruction directive. This would cause unnecessary delay in providing appropriate care and does not respect the legal standing of the directive.

The key principle is that a valid instruction directive made by a person with decision-making capacity must be followed, even if healthcare professionals or family members disagree with the decision. The only exceptions are if circumstances have changed significantly since the directive was made, or if there is evidence that the person had revoked the directive while they still had capacity.
learningPoints: Instruction directives are legally binding documents that specify a person's wishes for future medical care when they lose decision-making capacity.
Healthcare professionals must follow valid instruction directives unless circumstances have changed significantly since the directive was made.
Instruction directives can be revoked by a person with decision-making capacity at any time.
references: NIH National Institute on Aging. Advance Care Planning: Advance Directives for Health Care. National Institue of Aging. 2022. https://www.nia.nih.gov/health/advance-care-planning/advance-care-planning-advance-directives-hea...

SA Health. Appointing Substitute Decision-Makers. Government of South Australia -SA Health. 2024. Available at: https://www.sahealth.sa.gov.au/wps/wcm/connect/public+content/sa+health+internet/conditions/end+of+life+care/advance+care+directives/create+my+advance+care+directive/appointing+substitute+decision+makers
